content.text: TypeScript class inheritance follows JavaScript's prototype chain with added compile-time safety. A subclass uses extends to inherit all public and protected members from the base class, and can override methods using the override keyword. TypeScript checks that overriding methods are compatible with the base signature.

attrs.alt: Inheritance hierarchy: Animal base class with Dog and Cat subclasses each overriding makeSound() and adding their own members

content.text: super has two uses in a subclass: calling the base class constructor and calling a base class method. TypeScript requires super() in every subclass constructor before accessing this.
TypeScript 4.3 introduced the override keyword. When used, TypeScript verifies that the method actually exists in the base class — catching typos and method removal in the base class at compile time.
An abstract class cannot be instantiated directly. It defines methods that subclasses must implement (abstract methods) and may also provide concrete implementations shared by all subclasses.
Call base constructor or base method
Declares class cannot be instantiated, or method must be overridden
Enforce interface contract on the class
When a subclass adds new parameters, the constructor must call super() with the parameters the base class expects. The subclass may add its own parameters before or after the base ones.

Because a subclass instance IS-A base class instance, you can store subclass objects in base-typed collections and call overridden methods — TypeScript dispatches to the correct implementation at runtime.

Inheritance is appropriate when the subclass truly IS-A specialisation of the base class and needs to share most of its implementation. Prefer composition when you only want to reuse one or two behaviours.

Angular decorators (@Component, @Directive) are NOT inherited. When a component class extends a base, only the class members (methods, fields) are inherited — the parent's template, selector, and decorator metadata are not.
. A subclass inherits all public and protected members and can add or override them.
calls the base class constructor and must appear before
calls the base implementation of an overridden method.
verifies at compile time that the method exists in the base class — prevents silent typos and broken overrides when the base changes.
define a template with abstract methods that subclasses must implement. They cannot be instantiated directly but are valid as types.
members are accessible in the class and all subclasses but not outside the hierarchy.
In Angular, inheritance is most useful for abstract base services with shared HTTP or signal logic that concrete resource services extend.
